title: Laburnum linneanum
taxonomy: Laburnum linneanum is a member of the pea family, Fabaceae. It belongs to the subfamily Faboideae and tribe Genisteae. The plant's classification is further divided into the genus Laburnum and species L. x watereri (a hybrid of L. alpinum and L. anagyroides), however, L. alpinum is a species within the same genus. The classification may also reflect the different varieties, but the exact classification of L. linneanum is less clear in scientific literature. Some sources suggest L. linneanum may not be a valid or accepted name.
uses: Laburnum linneanum, also known as Irish Laburnum, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's seeds and pods are particularly valued for their potential medicinal properties. In traditional remedies, the seeds are said to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate skin conditions and reduce swelling. Additionally, the plant is believed to have astringent properties, which could be beneficial in treating diarrhea and other gastrointestinal issues. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Laburnum linneanum for medicinal purposes.
benefits: Laburnum linneanum, commonly known as Irish laburnum or golden chain tree, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant contains alkaloids, such as cytisine and lupanine, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. These properties may help to alleviate symptoms of respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma. Additionally, the plant's extract has been used to treat skin conditions and wounds due to its antibacterial and antifungal properties. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's health benefits and potential side effects.

constituents: Laburnum alpinum is actually the plant I believe you meant to ask about, not Laburnum linneanum (Laburnum linneanum is the alpine form of the Laburnum x watereri). side_effects: Laburnum linneanum, also known as Irish Laburnum or Golden Chain, contains toxic compounds called cytisine and linamarin, which can be harmful if ingested. If ingested, these compounds can cause nausea, vomiting, and other gastrointestinal symptoms. In severe cases, exposure to the plant's toxins can lead to more serious health issues, including seizures and even death. The seeds of the plant are particularly toxic and should be avoided altogether. Handling the plant's seeds or sap can also cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals.
intro: Laburnum linneanum, commonly known as the Irish laburnum or golden rain, is a small deciduous tree or shrub native to Ireland and the UK. It has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antispasmodic properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of anxiety, insomnia, and menstrual cramps. The bioactive constituents of Laburnum linneanum include alkaloids, such as cytisine and laburnine, which have been shown to exhibit neuroprotective and antioxidant effects. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include tinctures, teas, and infusions, which are often used to calm the nervous system and promote relaxation.
